section.title: The Word of Life
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens without a normal greeting, plunging straight into the eternal life that appeared in Jesus. John writes as an eyewitness who heard, saw, and touched the Word of life, and he proclaims Him so that his readers may share the same fellowship with the Father and the Son.
section.thesis: The eternal life that was with the Father has appeared in a real, touchable Jesus, and the apostles proclaim what they saw and heard so that the readers may share their fellowship with the Father and the Son and so that joy may be complete.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the eyewitness testimony to the Word of life; the purpose, fellowship with the apostles, the Father, and the Son; and the aim, joy made complete.

units.label: The Word of life we have seen

verses.text: That which was from {{arche|the beginning}}, which we have heard, which we have seen with our own eyes, which we have {{theaomai|gazed upon}} and touched with our own hands, this is the {{logos|Word}} of life.
And this is the {{zoe|life}} that was revealed; we have seen it and testified to it, and we proclaim to you the {{aionios|eternal}} life that was with the Father and was revealed to us.

units.label: Proclaimed for fellowship
units.note: with the Father and the Son

verses.text: We proclaim to you what we have seen and heard, so that you also may have {{koinonia|fellowship}} with us. And this fellowship of ours is with the Father and with His Son, Jesus Christ.
We write these things so that our {{chara|joy}} may be complete.

arche.gloss: Beginning, origin, first. John opens with the same phrase as his Gospel, that which was from the beginning. It reaches back before creation to the eternal Son who was already with the Father, the life that has no start and no end.

theaomai.gloss: To gaze upon, to behold with attention, to contemplate. It is stronger than a passing glance. The apostles did not merely notice Jesus; they beheld Him closely over time, which is why their testimony that the Word came in real flesh can be trusted.

logos.gloss: Word, message, the self-expression of God. Here it is the Word of life, echoing John 1:1 where the Word was with God and was God. The eternal Word became touchable in Jesus, so that the very life of God could be heard, seen, and handled.

zoe.gloss: Life, and in John especially the life of God shared with His people. This life is not an idea but a person; it was revealed, seen, and testified to. To have the Son is to have this life, a theme the whole letter returns to.

aionios.gloss: Eternal, everlasting, belonging to the age to come. The eternal life was with the Father and has now appeared. It is not only endless duration but a quality of life, knowing God, that begins now for those who believe.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, sharing, partnership, communion. It is a shared common life, not mere friendliness. The gospel is proclaimed so that hearers may be drawn into the apostles' fellowship, which is itself fellowship with the Father and the Son.

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness. John writes so that joy may be complete, filled to the full. True Christian joy is not manufactured but flows from real fellowship with God, and it grows as the readers are secured in the truth about Christ.

basic.context: John does not begin with hellos and names. He begins with Jesus. The eternal life that always existed with the Father stepped into history, and John and the other apostles heard Him, saw Him, and touched Him with their hands. He writes to pass on that eyewitness testimony so his readers can share the same living fellowship with God, and so their joy will be full.
basic.hermeneutics: This opening sets the whole letter's aim: assurance and joy grounded in a real Jesus. Against teachers who denied that the Son truly came in the flesh, John stacks up the language of the senses, heard, seen, gazed upon, touched. The point is not a mood but a fact: the life of God appeared in a body you could touch.
basic.originalLanguages: That which was from the beginning, arche, is the eternal Word, logos, of life. The apostles gazed upon, theaomai, this life, zoe, which is eternal, aionios, and was with the Father. They proclaim Him so readers may share fellowship, koinonia, with the Father and the Son, and so that joy, chara, may be complete.
basic.typology: The Word who was from the beginning and became touchable answers the whole Old Testament longing for God to dwell with His people. The tabernacle and temple, where God met Israel behind a veil, give way to the Word made flesh whom the apostles could see and handle.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Word of life, the eternal life that was with the Father made visible and touchable. Everything John will say about light, love, and truth rests here: the Son truly came, was truly seen, and truly is the life of God given to us.
basic.god: God is not distant or unknowable. The Father sent His Son so that the eternal life could be revealed and shared. He desires fellowship with people and full joy for them, and He secures it by making Himself known in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: John's Gospel opens the same way, the Word was with God and was God. John 1:14 says the Word became flesh and we beheld His glory. Luke 24 and John 20 record the risen Jesus inviting the disciples to touch Him.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward God dwelling with His people. Here the eternal life steps into history in Jesus, is seen and touched, and is proclaimed so that people may be brought into fellowship with the Father and the Son, the very life the rest of Scripture promises.

context.literary: The prologue of the letter, deliberately echoing the prologue of John's Gospel. It is one long, weighty sentence in Greek, piling up verbs of perception before finally naming the Word of life, then stating the purpose, fellowship, and the aim, complete joy.
context.historical: Written to churches troubled by teachers who had left the fellowship and who denied that the Son had genuinely come in the flesh, ideas linked to early docetic and proto-gnostic thought. John, an aged eyewitness, answers with the physical reality of what he saw and touched.
context.canonical: It stands with John 1:1-18 and the resurrection accounts as a cornerstone of the church's confession that the eternal Son truly became man. It also frames the letter's tests of true faith, which all assume that the life of God has really appeared in Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the readers' faith and joy in the apostolic eyewitness testimony that the eternal life appeared in a real, touchable Jesus, and to draw them into fellowship with the Father and the Son.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These verses are chiefly testimony and statement of purpose rather than command. The abiding truths are that the Son truly came in the flesh, that the apostolic witness is trustworthy, and that God's aim in the gospel is shared fellowship and full joy.

debates.question: What does John mean by 'that which was from the beginning' and 'the Word of life'?

views.label: The eternal Son, the person of Christ
views.case: The parallels with John 1:1-14, where the Word is a person who was with God and became flesh, and the sensory verbs, heard, seen, touched, point to a person, not merely a message. The eternal life that was with the Father and was revealed is Jesus Himself. This is the strongest reading and it fits the letter's insistence on the incarnation.

views.label: The gospel message about Christ
views.case: Some stress that John proclaims a message and writes these things, so 'the Word of life' could mean the life-giving message rather than the person.

views.label: A deliberate blend of person and message
views.case: Others hold that John hovers between the person and the proclamation about Him, since the two cannot be separated in his thought.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading centres on the person of the eternal Son who became touchable in Jesus, while gladly affirming that the message about Him is inseparable from Him. Either way the incarnation is real and the apostolic testimony to it is sure, which is exactly the assurance John wants his readers to have.

study.originalLanguages: John reaches back to the arche, the beginning, the same word that opens his Gospel and the Greek Old Testament, to speak of the eternal Word, logos, of life. He piles up verbs of the senses, and the strongest of them, theaomai, means to gaze upon and contemplate, not merely to glimpse. What was gazed upon is the life, zoe, that is eternal, aionios, and was with the Father before appearing among us. All of it is proclaimed for one end, koinonia, a shared common life with the Father and the Son, and so that joy, chara, may be filled to the full.

typology.type: The Word who was from the beginning and became touchable
typology.fulfillment: The eternal Son, John 1:1-14, in whom God comes to dwell with His people, answering the tabernacle and temple where God met Israel behind a veil.

typology.type: The eternal life revealed and handled by the apostles
typology.fulfillment: The risen Christ who invited the disciples to see and touch Him, Luke 24:39, John 20:27, and who gives His life to all who believe.

study.christ: Jesus is the Word of life, the eternal life that was with the Father now made visible, audible, and touchable. John insists on the physical reality of the Son against any teaching that He only seemed to be human. The whole letter rests on this: because the Son truly came in the flesh, the life of God is really offered to us, and fellowship with the Father comes only through the Son, Jesus Christ.
study.god: God the Father is the source of the eternal life that appeared in the Son. He is not remote but self-giving, sending His Son so that people may know Him and share His life. His purpose in the gospel is fellowship, a shared common life with Himself, and joy made complete, which shows a God who delights to be known and to gladden His people.

crossReferences.note: The Word was with God and was God, and became flesh; the prologue John's letter deliberately echoes.
The risen Jesus invites Thomas to touch Him, grounding the apostolic testimony that the Son came in real flesh.
Touch Me and see; a spirit does not have flesh and bones, the same physical reality John proclaims.
God has given us eternal life, and this life is in His Son, the same eternal life named here and the aim of the whole letter.

study.oneStory: Scripture's one story runs toward God dwelling with His people in fullness of joy. Here the eternal life that was with the Father steps into history in Jesus, is truly seen and touched, and is proclaimed by eyewitnesses so that hearers may be drawn into fellowship with the Father and the Son. The life the whole Bible promises has a face and a name, and John writes so that our joy in Him may be complete.

sermon.title: The Word of Life
sermon.why: Begg preaches this opening, 1 John 1:1-4, on the eternal Word of life whom the apostles heard, saw, and touched, and on the fellowship and joy the gospel brings. A clear, orthodox handling of the letter's prologue.

quiz.q: What does John emphasize about the Word of life in verses 1 and 2?

options.text: That the eternal life was really heard, seen, and touched by the apostles.
options.note: John piles up verbs of the senses to insist the Son truly came in the flesh.

options.text: That the message about Jesus is inspiring even if His body was not real.
options.note: This denies the very point; John stresses the physical reality of the Son.

options.text: That Jesus began to exist when He was born.
options.note: John says He was from the beginning, with the Father, before appearing among us.

options.text: That Jesus was a great teacher whose words gave life.
options.note: His words do give life, but John's emphasis is that the eternal life itself appeared and was touched.

quiz.q: Why does John proclaim what he has seen and heard?

options.text: So that his readers may share fellowship with the apostles, the Father, and the Son.
options.note: Verse 3 names fellowship with the Father and His Son as the purpose.

options.text: So that his readers will admire the apostles' experiences.
options.note: The aim is not admiration but shared fellowship with God.

options.text: So that his readers may gain secret knowledge unavailable to others.
options.note: John opposes secret-knowledge teaching; the testimony is open and for all.

options.text: So that his readers will have accurate information about history.
options.note: The testimony is accurate, but the goal is fellowship, not information alone.

quiz.q: What is the stated aim of John's writing in verse 4?

options.text: That joy may be complete, filled to the full.
options.note: John writes so that joy, rooted in fellowship with God, may be made complete.

options.text: That the readers would feel guilty enough to change.
options.note: The aim named is full joy, not guilt, though the letter does call to holiness.

options.text: That the readers would fear losing their salvation daily.
options.note: The letter aims at assurance and joy, not anxious fear.

options.text: That the readers would enjoy a well-written letter.
options.note: The letter is well crafted, but the aim is joy grounded in fellowship with the Father and Son.

quiz.q: What does 'that which was from the beginning' most likely refer to?

options.text: The eternal Son who was with the Father before appearing in the flesh.
options.note: Echoing John 1:1, it points to the eternal Word, a person, now revealed in Jesus.

options.text: The life-giving message about Jesus, inseparable from Him.
options.note: The message is inseparable from Him, but the phrase centres on the eternal person.

options.text: The start of John's own ministry.
options.note: The beginning here is far earlier, reaching before creation to the Father.

options.text: The founding of the first church.
options.note: The reference is to the eternal Son, not the church's founding.

quiz.q: How is fellowship with God made possible, according to these verses?

options.text: Through the Son, Jesus Christ, whom the apostles proclaim.
options.note: Verse 3 ties fellowship with the Father to fellowship with His Son, Jesus Christ.

options.text: Through sincere effort to be a good person.
options.note: Fellowship comes through the Son, not through self-improvement.

options.text: Through private mystical experiences apart from Christ.
options.note: John grounds fellowship in the proclaimed Son, not in experiences that bypass Him.

options.text: Through belonging to the right community.
options.note: The apostolic fellowship matters, but it is fellowship with the Father and the Son that is decisive.

questions: John insists that the eternal life was heard, seen, and touched. Why does it matter for your faith and hope that Jesus was truly, physically real and not merely an idea?
The gospel is proclaimed so that we may have fellowship with the Father and the Son. How would you describe the difference between knowing about God and sharing fellowship with Him?
John writes so that joy may be complete. Where does your joy tend to come from, and how might it be deepened by fellowship with God rather than circumstances?
The apostles passed on what they had seen and heard. Who passed the testimony of Christ on to you, and to whom might you pass it on?
This eternal life was with the Father from the beginning and has now appeared. How does the eternity and reality of Christ steady you when your faith feels shaky?

translationNotes.note: The opening string of relative clauses has no clear subject until 'the Word of life', building suspense; the neuter 'that which' embraces both the eternal person and all that was revealed in Him.
The BSB reads 'touched with our own hands' where the Greek uses a verb meaning to feel or handle, the same idea as the risen Jesus inviting the disciples to handle Him in Luke 24:39.
Some manuscripts read 'your joy' rather than 'our joy', as the BSB footnote notes; either way the joy flows from shared fellowship and is meant to be complete.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 1:1-4. The em dash in verse 1 ('our own hands, this is the Word of life') is rendered as a comma per Makor style.
